1: A type: the righteous sufferer crying to God while hemmed in by hostile forces. The pattern reaches its apex in Christ — Gethsemane ("My soul is very sorrowful, even to death", Mark 14:34) and the cross (Matthew 27:46 = Psalms 22:1). The cry that God hears in distress is the same cry Christ made as the embodiment of Israel in extremity, and God answered it through the resurrection.
2: A theme: deliverance from the deceitful tongue. Christ stood surrounded by false witnesses at his trial (Matthew 26:59-60; Mark 14:55-56), fulfilling the pattern of the innocent one falsely accused. He is the Truth (John 14:6) opposed by the father of lies (John 8:44); his vindication by the resurrection is the definitive answer to the lying lips that sought his death.
3: A revelation of God: the God of just recompense whose punishment matches the crime. Christ will execute this final justice at his return — 2 Thessalonians 2:8 ("slain by the breath of his mouth"); Revelation 19:11-15. The God who hates the lying tongue is the God who entrusted all judgment to the Son (John 5:22).
4: A revelation of God: warrior arrows and burning coals as instruments of divine judgment on the deceiver. This belongs to the eschatological justice Christ will execute — the same Christ who warned that every careless word will be judged (Matthew 12:36) and who is described in Revelation 19:15 as striking the nations with a sharp sword from his mouth.
5: A theme: the exile condition — dwelling among those hostile to God. Christ was himself an alien in a world that rejected him: John 1:11 ("he came to his own, and his own people did not receive him"). The pilgrim psalm sung toward Jerusalem is the prayer of one who longs for the true homeland, which Hebrews 11:16 identifies as the heavenly country that God has prepared.
6: A theme: "too long have I had my dwelling among those who hate peace." Christ dwelt his entire ministry among those who would ultimately reject and crucify him. Luke 19:42 — he wept over Jerusalem because it "did not know the things that make for peace." His presence was the peace they refused.
7: A direct: "I am for peace, but when I speak, they are for war." The Prince of Peace (Isaiah 9:6) came preaching peace (Luke 19:38; Ephesians 2:17 — "he came and preached peace to you who were far off") and was met with hostility. Ephesians 2:14 — "he himself is our peace, who has made the two groups one." The verse prefigures the reception of the Messiah whose peace proclamation was answered with crucifixion.
